title: Country Risk 2025: The Story behind the Numbers!
gist: Damodaran breaks down the four qualitative drivers of country risk (political structure, violence, corruption, legal systems) and walks through how he converts sovereign ratings, CDS spreads and the implied US equity risk premium into country-by-country equity risk premiums, updated for Moody's May 2025 downgrade of the US from Aaa to Aa1.

insights.claim: Democracies carry continuous policy risk because newly elected governments can revisit or discard prior policies, while authoritarian regimes offer long-term policy continuity punctuated by infrequent but wrenching policy shifts when the government does change.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not treat 'democracy = low risk' as a given; underwrite the specific type of policy risk (continuous vs. discontinuous) each regime type poses to an operating business.
insights.quote: an authoritarian government can provide long term policy continuity, with the catch being that changes in the government, though infrequent, can create wrenching policy shifts that businesses have to learn to live with
insights.locator: I will start with political structure

insights.claim: According to the Economist Intelligence Unit's democracy data heading into 2025, a large proportion of the world remains under authoritarian rule and the global trend over the last decade has moved toward more authoritarianism, not less.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A secular drift toward authoritarianism raises the average discontinuous-policy-shift risk investors should price into emerging and frontier market equity premiums over time.
insights.quote: not only is a large proportion of the world under authoritarian rule, but the trend lines on this dimension also have been towards more authoritarianism in the last decade
insights.locator: Source: Economist Intelligence Unit (EIU)

insights.claim: Per the Institute for Economics & Peace's 2024 exposure-to-violence data, the United States and Brazil score as more exposed to violence than Argentina and Chile, and India scores as more exposed than China.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Violence-exposure rankings don't track conventional emerging-vs-developed intuition, so investors relying on GDP-per-capita or credit-rating proxies for security risk may be mispricing it in specific country pairs.
insights.quote: the United States and Brazil have higher exposures to violence than Argentina and Chile, and India has more exposure to violence than China
insights.locator: Institute of Economics & Peace

insights.claim: Corruption functions as an implicit tax on businesses that reduces profits, cash flows and value, and it also structurally disadvantages honest operators who are unwilling to match less scrupulous competitors' rule-breaking.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: When modeling margins for businesses in high-corruption jurisdictions, treat corruption as a recurring cost line (implicit tax) rather than a one-off risk, and expect compliant multinationals to underperform local incumbents on cost structure.
insights.quote: even for those businesses that are willing to play the corruption game, it creates costs that I would liken to an implicit tax that reduces profits, cash flows and value
insights.locator: Corruption is a concern for everyone

insights.claim: Corruption tends to arise systematically wherever bureaucratic licensing and approval regimes exist and the bureaucrats granting licenses are paid far less than the businesses they regulate.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors can use regulatory/licensing density as a leading indicator of corruption risk in a jurisdiction, rather than waiting for lagging corruption indices to update.
insights.quote: corruption almost inevitably follows in bureaucratic settings, where you need licenses and approvals for even the most trivial of actions, and the bureaucrats...are paid a pittance relative to the businesses that they regulate
insights.locator: As you can see from the map

insights.claim: Legal system quality for property rights and contract enforcement depends not just on the laws on the books but on how quickly they are enforced, since long delays in justice are almost as damaging as a biased or capricious system.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: When assessing legal risk in a country, investors should check enforcement timelines/court backlogs, not just the formal legal code, before assuming property rights are secure.
insights.quote: a legal system where justice is delayed for years or even decades is almost as bad as one that is capricious and biased
insights.locator: As a final component, I look at legal systems

themes.theme: How sovereign risk is measured, and where each measure breaks down

insights.claim: The number of countries with Moody's sovereign ratings grew from just 13 in 1985 to 143 in 2025, but the count of Aaa-rated countries has stayed roughly flat and actually fell from fifteen a decade ago to eleven today, with the UK and France both losing their Aaa ratings in that period.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The shrinking pool of 'riskless' sovereigns means fewer true Aaa benchmarks are available globally, pushing more of the investable universe into some positive country-risk premium.
insights.quote: Moody's rated 13 countries in 1985, but that number increased to 143 in 2025... the number of Aaa rated countries stayed at eleven... has dropped from fifteen just a decade ago, with the UK and France losing their Aaa ratings
insights.locator: The simplest and most longstanding measure of country risk

insights.claim: Moody's downgraded the United States from Aaa to Aa1 in May 2025, bringing it in line with S&P (which downgraded the US in 2011) and Fitch (which downgraded it in 2023).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The US Treasury rate can no longer be treated as a pure default-free rate in valuation models; a default spread must now be netted out, which is a structural change analysts using US-anchored DCFs need to adopt.
insights.quote: In May 2025, Moody's downgraded the United States, bringing them in line with the other ratings agencies; S&P downgraded the US in 2011 and Fitch in 2023
insights.locator: The heat map below captures sovereign ratings

insights.claim: Sovereign credit ratings can mislead as overall country-risk measures because their narrow focus on default risk causes them to understate political risk in places like the Middle East, which carries high ratings despite elevated political risk, and rating agencies have been critiqued for overrating European countries while underrating Asian, African and Latin American countries, with ratings changes also lagging events on the ground.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't rely on sovereign ratings alone for country risk in politically volatile but fiscally strong regions (e.g. Middle East); supplement with CDS spreads or political risk scores.
insights.quote: their focus on default risk can lead them to be misleading measures of overall country risk, especially in countries that have political risk issues but not much default risk; the Middle East, for instance, has high sovereign ratings
insights.locator: While sovereign ratings are useful risk measures

insights.claim: As of end-June 2025, roughly 80 countries had actively traded sovereign CDS, which reprice default risk almost instantaneously but with more volatility than credit ratings, and Damodaran uses CDS and ratings together to triangulate sovereign default risk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: For real-time country risk monitoring (e.g. reacting to a fiscal or political shock), sovereign CDS spreads are a faster signal than waiting for a rating-agency action.
insights.quote: At the end of June 2025, there were about 80 countries with sovereign CDS available on them
insights.locator: If you are leery about trusting ratings agencies

insights.claim: Broader country risk scoring services such as Political Risk Services (PRS) produce counterintuitive rankings — PRS scores the United States as riskier than Vietnam and Libya — which is one reason these composite country-risk-score products have never gained wide traction in valuation practice.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat composite third-party country risk scores skeptically and cross-check them against ratings/CDS before using them to size a country risk premium.
insights.quote: There are some puzzling numbers here, with the United States coming in as riskier than Vietnam and Libya, but that is one reason why country risk scores have never acquired traction
insights.locator: If default risk seems like to provide too narrow a focus

themes.theme: Rebuilding the equity risk premium after the US downgrade

insights.claim: Damodaran's implied equity risk premium for the S&P 500, derived by solving for the discount rate that equates the present value of expected dividends, buybacks and their growth to the index level, came out to an expected return of 8.45% as of June 30, 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This 8.45% expected-return figure is the market-implied hurdle rate input investors can compare against their own return assumptions when judging whether US equities are cheap or expensive.
insights.quote: On June 30, 2025, this assessment generated an expected return of 8.45% for the index
insights.locator: The process starts with my estimate of the implied equity risk premium

insights.claim: Because Moody's downgrade means the US Treasury rate now embeds a default spread, Damodaran now computes the dollar riskfree rate as the T-Bond rate minus the Aa1 default spread (4.24% - 0.27% = 3.97%), yielding an implied US equity risk premium of 4.48% (8.45% - 3.97%) and a mature-market premium of 4.21% after stripping out the US's own default spread.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Any analyst still using the raw 10-year Treasury yield as the riskfree rate in a US DCF is now overstating the riskfree rate and mispricing the true cost of equity by roughly 27bps embedded default risk.
insights.quote: Riskfree rate in US dollars = T.Bond rate minus Default Spread for Aa1 rating = 4.24% - 0.27% = 3.97%... Implied equity risk premium for US...= 8.45% - 3.97% = 4.48%
insights.locator: Until May 2025, I just subtracted the US 10-year treasury bond rate

insights.claim: As a direct consequence of the US losing its Aaa rating, the United States now carries a higher equity risk premium than the remaining Aaa-rated countries in Northern Europe, Australia and Canada.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Relative valuation comparisons between US equities and other Aaa-market equities should now bake in a structurally higher US country-risk component than pre-2025 models assumed.
insights.quote: The effects of the US ratings downgrade also manifest in the table, with the US now having a higher equity risk premium than its Aaa counterparts in Northern Europe, Australia and Canada
insights.locator: If you compare the equity risk premium heat map

insights.claim: Country-level equity risk premiums track closely with the other country-risk heat maps (political structure, violence exposure, corruption, legal quality) — the parts of the world most exposed to corruption, violence and weak legal enforcement also carry the highest equity risk premiums.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Confirms that the quantitative ERP framework is internally consistent with the qualitative risk dimensions, so investors can use corruption/violence/legal-quality data as a sanity check on any country ERP number they're handed.
insights.quote: The parts of the world that are most exposed to corruption and violence, and have capricious legal systems, tend to have higher equity risk premiums
insights.locator: If you compare the equity risk premium heat map

insights.claim: As of July 2025, investors need to earn 7.46 percentage points above the riskfree rate for an average-risk investment in India, and 10.87 percentage points above the riskfree rate for an average-risk investment in Turkey.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: These are concrete, usable country risk premium inputs for anyone building a cost-of-equity estimate for Indian or Turkish operating assets right now.
insights.quote: investors need to earn 7.46% more than the riskfree rate to invest in an average-risk investment in India, and 10.87% more than the riskfree rate to invest in an average risk investment in Turkey
insights.locator: Using the numbers from July 2025

insights.claim: Equity risk premiums and stock prices move inversely: the ERP rises as stock prices drop and falls as stock prices rise, holding other things constant.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A rising implied ERP during a selloff is not necessarily a red flag — it's the market repricing itself to demand more compensation for risk, useful as a real-time gauge of market-wide risk aversion.
insights.quote: equity risk premiums and stock prices move inversely, with the ERP rising as stock prices drop (all other thins being held constant) and falling as stock prices increase
insights.locator: It is also worth recognizing how equity risk premiums play out

themes.theme: A practitioner's playbook for applying country ERPs in valuation

insights.claim: Country risk exposure should be assigned based on where a company actually operates (via revenue or production weighting by geography), not where it is legally incorporated, which opens the door to bringing emerging-market country risk into the cost of equity for multinationals nominally domiciled in mature markets.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts valuing 'developed market' multinationals with large emerging-market revenue exposure should blend in EM country risk premiums rather than defaulting to the home-country ERP — mispricing this understates true cost of capital.
insights.quote: the exposure to country risk comes from where a company operates, not where it is incorporated... When Amazon decides to invest in a Brazilian online retail project, it is the equity risk premium for Brazil that should be incorporated
insights.locator: Step 3: Estimate the equity risk premium

insights.claim: For a non-Aaa country, the correct riskfree rate in that country's currency is the government bond rate minus that country's own default spread; using the raw government bond rate instead double-counts country risk, once in the bond rate and again in the equity risk premium.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A quick diagnostic for spotting flawed DCFs: if an analyst uses a local government bond yield unadjusted as the riskfree rate while also adding a country ERP on top, they are systematically overstating the discount rate.
insights.quote: if using the Indian government bond rate as the riskfree rate in rupees, you would effectively be double counting Indian country risk, once in the government bond rate and once again in the equity risk premium
insights.locator: Staying with the example of an Indian investment

insights.claim: For an Indian rupee investment on July 1, 2025, using an Indian government bond rate of 6.32% minus a 2.16% default spread gives a rupee riskfree rate of 4.16%, which combined with India's 7.46% ERP yields an expected return of 11.62% on an average-risk Indian equity.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is a directly reusable worked cost-of-equity calculation for any rupee-denominated Indian equity or project as of July 2025.
insights.quote: Indian government bond rate on July 1, 2025 = 6.32%... Default spread for India...= 2.16%... Indian rupee risk free rate...= 4.16%... Expected return on average Indian equity in rupees...= 11.62%
insights.locator: Indian government bond rate on July 1, 2025

insights.claim: For high-inflation currencies like the Brazilian real or Turkish lira, it is more prudent to compute the expected return entirely in US dollars and then convert into the local currency using the inflation differential, rather than directly adding the dollar-based ERP to a local bond rate; for Turkey this produced an expected USD return of 14.84% but a lira-denominated expected return of 34.45% once the 20% Turkish inflation assumption (versus 2.5% US inflation) is layered in.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: When valuing assets in high-inflation emerging markets, expect nominal discount rates and growth rates to both scale up sharply with local inflation — comparing a 14.84% USD return to a raw local bond yield without this adjustment will misstate risk.
insights.quote: Expected return on average Turkish equity in US $ on July 1, 2025 = 3.97% + 10.87% = 14.84%... Expected return on average Turkish equity Turkish lira on July 1, 2025 = 1.1484 *(1.20/1.025) -1 = 34.45%
insights.locator: For currencies, like the Brazilian real or Turkish lira

insights.claim: Cost of equity is computed as the riskfree rate plus beta times the equity risk premium, and industry-average or bottom-up betas produce materially better estimates for companies and projects than betas derived from regressions against market indices.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts should default to bottom-up/industry-average betas rather than trailing regression betas when estimating cost of equity, since regression betas are noisy and less reliable for individual companies.
insights.quote: using industry-average or bottom-up betas yields much better estimates for projects and companies
insights.locator: Cost of equity = Riskfree rate + Beta

insights.claim: Cost of debt equals the riskfree rate plus the company's own default spread, and a company operating in a sovereign-risk country often bears a double burden of carrying both the country's default spread (embedded in the riskfree rate) and its own company-specific default spread.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: When estimating financing costs for companies in weaker sovereigns, don't just apply a corporate credit spread on top of the raw government bond yield — the sovereign risk is already layered into the adjusted riskfree rate.
insights.quote: a company in a country with sovereign default risk will often bear a double burden, carrying default spreads for both itself and the country
insights.locator: Step 4: Estimate project-specific or company-specific risk measures

insights.claim: The currency chosen for valuation must be applied consistently to both the discount rate and the cash flow growth assumptions, since both are driven by the same expected inflation rate — a Turkish lira project should show a discount rate above 25% alongside correspondingly inflated cash flow growth, whereas the same project analyzed in euros would show a much lower discount rate but muted nominal growth.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A common valuation error is mixing a high local discount rate with cash flows that haven't been inflated to match — always keep the currency/inflation assumption consistent across the discount rate and the numerator.
insights.quote: if you analyze a Turkish project in lira, where the expected inflation rate is 20%, you should expect to see costs of equity and capital that exceed 25%, but you should also see growth rates in the cash flows to be inflated the same expected inflation
insights.locator: Step 5: Ensure that your cash flows are currency consistent
